question: Which product categories are performing best and which are struggling?
sql: SELECT p.category, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS total_orders, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price), 2) AS total_revenue, ROUND(AVG(oi.sale_price), 2) AS avg_order_value, COUNTIF(oi.status = 'Returned') AS returned_items, ROUND(COUNTIF(oi.status = 'Returned') / COUNT(*) * 100, 1) AS return_rate_pct, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price - p.cost), 2) AS total_profit FROM `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.order_items` oi JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.orders` o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.products` p ON oi.product_id = p.id GROUP BY p.category ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
report: Outerwear & Coats leads revenue at $1.35M despite ranking 3rd in order volume, driven by the highest average order value ($146) — nearly 4x that of Intimates ($34). This makes Outerwear the most efficient category by revenue-per-order ($150 profit vs $17 for Intimates). Return rates are strikingly uniform across all categories (9-11%), suggesting returns are driven by systemic factors such as sizing policy or logistics, rather than category-specific quality issues. Clothing Sets is the exception at 13.9% return rate and warrants investigation. Jeans generates strong revenue ($1.26M) but at lower margin efficiency than Outerwear — $47 profit per order vs $83. Intimates drives the highest transaction volume (12,611 orders) but at the lowest AOV, making it a volume play rather than a value driver. Recommendation: prioritize Outerwear & Coats for marketing investment given its superior revenue efficiency.

question: How has our revenue trended over the last 12 months? Is the business growing?
sql: SELECT FORMAT_TIMESTAMP('%Y-%m', o.created_at) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price), 2) AS revenue, COUNT(DISTINCT o.user_id) AS unique_customers, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price) / COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id), 2) AS avg_order_value FROM `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.orders` o JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.order_items` oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE DATE(o.created_at) >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 12 MONTH) AND o.status NOT IN ('Cancelled', 'Returned') GROUP BY month ORDER BY month
report: Revenue shows strong and consistent growth over the trailing 12 months: from $186K in July 2025 to $375K in May 2026 — a 101% year-over-year increase. Critically, average order value remained stable throughout at $84-90, meaning all growth is volume-driven: more customers ordering, not customers spending more per order. This signals healthy top-of-funnel acquisition rather than price inflation. Customer acquisition accelerated notably in March 2026 (3,400 unique customers vs 2,747 in February — a 24% jump), suggesting a successful campaign or seasonal tailwind. The business is on a clear upward trajectory with no signs of plateau. Recommendation: investigate the March 2026 acquisition spike to identify the driver and replicate it.

question: Who are our most valuable customers and what do they buy?
sql: SELECT u.id AS user_id, u.country, u.age, u.gender, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS total_orders, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price), 2) AS lifetime_value, ROUND(AVG(oi.sale_price), 2) AS avg_order_value, STRING_AGG(DISTINCT p.category ORDER BY p.category LIMIT 3) AS top_categories FROM `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.users` u JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.orders` o ON u.id = o.user_id JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.order_items` oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.products` p ON oi.product_id = p.id WHERE o.status = 'Complete' GROUP BY u.id, u.country, u.age, u.gender ORDER BY lifetime_value DESC LIMIT 20
report: Top 20 customers by lifetime value range from $1,026 to $1,559, with most completing only 1-2 orders at high average order values ($200-560). This reveals a critical insight: the high-value customer segment is dominated by single high-ticket purchases rather than loyal repeat buyers — a significant retention opportunity. China, the US, and Brasil dominate the top 20 by country. Outerwear & Coats appears in the top category mix for the majority of high-value customers, reinforcing its role as the premium anchor category that attracts high-spending customers. The age range is broad (18-68), suggesting no strong demographic concentration in the high-value segment. Recommendation: implement a post-purchase retention program targeting customers with one completed high-value order, as converting them to repeat buyers would disproportionately impact revenue.

question: Why is our return rate high? Which brands and categories are driving it?
sql: SELECT p.brand, p.category, COUNT(*) AS total_items_sold, COUNTIF(oi.status = 'Returned') AS returned, ROUND(COUNTIF(oi.status = 'Returned') / COUNT(*) * 100, 1) AS return_rate_pct, ROUND(AVG(p.retail_price), 2) AS avg_price, ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN oi.status = 'Returned' THEN oi.sale_price ELSE 0 END), 2) AS lost_revenue FROM `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.order_items` oi JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.products` p ON oi.product_id = p.id GROUP BY p.brand, p.category HAVING total_items_sold > 50 ORDER BY return_rate_pct DESC LIMIT 20
report: The overall 10% return rate masks significant brand-level variation. Southpole (Tops & Tees, 24.1%) and HoodieBuddie (Fashion Hoodies, 23%) have return rates more than double the category average, despite below-average price points ($25 and $45) — indicating quality or sizing inconsistency rather than price remorse. Premium brands also struggle: Oakley (Plus category, avg $145) at 18.7% generates $2,370 in lost revenue — the highest single-brand loss on this list. True Religion (Pants, avg $147) similarly at 17.3% and $1,292 lost. This dual pattern reveals two distinct return drivers: (1) quality and sizing problems in budget brands, (2) expectation-reality mismatch in premium brands, likely due to inadequate product descriptions or imagery. Recommendation: audit Southpole and HoodieBuddie supplier contracts; improve product photography and size guides for Oakley and True Religion listings.

question: Which countries are our strongest markets and how do they compare?
sql: SELECT u.country, COUNT(DISTINCT u.id) AS customers, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price), 2) AS revenue, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price) / COUNT(DISTINCT u.id), 2) AS revenue_per_customer, ROUND(COUNTIF(o.status = 'Returned') / COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) * 100, 1) AS return_rate_pct FROM `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.users` u JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.orders` o ON u.id = o.user_id JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.order_items` oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id GROUP BY u.country ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 15
report: China and the United States are the dominant markets by revenue ($3.6M and $2.4M respectively), with Brasil a distant third at $1.6M. Revenue per customer is remarkably consistent across geographies ($129-160), suggesting purchasing behavior is globally homogeneous and pricing strategy is working uniformly. Poland is the notable exception at $115/customer — a potentially price-sensitive market worth monitoring or targeting with promotions. Return rates are elevated in Germany (15.6%) and the UK (15.2%) versus the ~14% global average; this likely reflects stronger consumer protection regulations enabling easier returns rather than product dissatisfaction. Colombia shows a 26.7% return rate but with only 9 customers — statistically insignificant and should not drive policy decisions. Recommendation: growth efforts should prioritize Brasil and South Korea, which combine large customer bases with average return rates, offering the best expansion risk-reward profile.
